content.full_text:
I 
File -
Gov. Code Section 84210(g) 
Date 
April 2, 1979 
from 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Barbara Campbel~C 
~IJ'JI""'" Telephone advice to Kirk Pessner 
A candidate used someone's contry club membership to hold a 
fundraiser dinner at the country club. 
The candidate reimbursed 
the club member for all the costs of the dinner. 
Kirk Pessner 
asked on March 30, 1979, if the use of the country club member-
ship constituted an in-kind contribution and, if so, what was 
the value. 
After discussing the matter with other staff members, we 
concluded the club's procedure for use of rooms would determine 
if there was an in-kind contribution. 
If, as a part of the member-
ship, a club member can use the facilities of the club without 
charge as long as the member pays for the services, there will 
be an in-kind contribution of the fair market falue of the room 
or an equivalent room. 
If, however, the member has to pay to 
use the facilities, the candidate would reimburse the club member 
for the room fee and report that payment on the campaign statement. 
I spoke with Kirk and relayed the above advice. 
According 
to Kirk, the country club in question allows its members to use 
the facilities free of charge. 
Therefore I advised him that the 
member made an in-kind contribution of the fair market value of 
the rental of the room or its equivalent. 
BC:plh

sections.question_synthetic: Does a candidate's use of someone's country club membership to hold a fundraiser dinner, where the candidate reimburses all costs, constitute an in-kind contribution, and if so, how should it be valued?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: If club members can use facilities free of charge as part of membership (only paying for services), the member made an in-kind contribution equal to the fair market value of the room rental. If members must pay to use facilities, the candidate should reimburse the member for the room fee and report it on the campaign statement. In this case, the country club allows free member use, so an in-kind contribution of fair market value of the room applies.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is a telephone advice letter with no formal question/conclusion sections; content extracted from narrative body. Date April 2, 1979; advice given to Kirk Pessner regarding call on March 30, 1979. Minor typo in document ('contry' instead of 'country', 'falue' instead of 'value') preserved as in original.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does a candidate's use of someone's country club membership to hold a fundraiser dinner, where the candidate reimburses all costs, constitute an in-kind contribution, and if so, how should it be valued?

CONCLUSION: If club members can use facilities free of charge as part of membership (only paying for services), the member made an in-kind contribution equal to the fair market value of the room rental. If members must pay to use facilities, the candidate should reimburse the member for the room fee and report it on the campaign statement. In this case, the country club allows free member use, so an in-kind contribution of fair market value of the room applies.
embedding.first_500_words: I File - Gov. Code Section 84210(g) Date April 2, 1979 from FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION Barbara Campbel~C ~IJ'JI""'" Telephone advice to Kirk Pessner A candidate used someone's contry club membership to hold a fundraiser dinner at the country club. The candidate reimbursed the club member for all the costs of the dinner. Kirk Pessner asked on March 30, 1979, if the use of the country club member- ship constituted an in-kind contribution and, if so, what was the value. After discussing the matter with other staff members, we concluded the club's procedure for use of rooms would determine if there was an in-kind contribution. If, as a part of the member- ship, a club member can use the facilities of the club without charge as long as the member pays for the services, there will be an in-kind contribution of the fair market falue of the room or an equivalent room. If, however, the member has to pay to use the facilities, the candidate would reimburse the club member for the room fee and report that payment on the campaign statement. I spoke with Kirk and relayed the above advice. According to Kirk, the country club in question allows its members to use the facilities free of charge. Therefore I advised him that the member made an in-kind contribution of the fair market value of the rental of the room or its equivalent. BC:plh
embedding.summary: FPPC provided telephone advice regarding whether a candidate's use of a country club member's membership to host a fundraiser constituted an in-kind contribution. The determination depends on the club's membership privileges: if members use facilities free (paying only for services), it is an in-kind contribution valued at fair market room rental; if members must pay fees, the candidate reimburses the member and reports the payment.
